coredata.dc:title: Carbon dot embedded photonic crystal molecularly imprinted as dual-mode fluorometric/colorimetric sensor for the determination of sulfadimethoxine in fish
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Food Composition and Analysis
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Shishun
Shao, Keman
Hong, Chengyi
Chen, Suyan
Lin, Zhengzhong
Huang, Zhiyong
Mureti, Gulinigaer
coredata.dc:description: A novel fluorescent membrane based on carbon dot embedded photonic crystal molecularly imprinted polymer (PCMIP@CDs) was developed for fluorometric and colorimetric dual-mode determination of sulfadimethoxine with excellent selectivity, quick response, and reusability. In the presence of sulfadimethoxine, fluorescence quenching and red shift of diffraction peak from PCMIP@CDs were observed because of the photo-induced electron transfer and structural change of PCMIP@CDs, accompanied by a visual color change from blue to green. The fluorescence quenching efficiency and the red-shift of the diffraction peaks were positively correlated with the logarithms of the sulfadimethoxine concentration at 10−12–10−5 and 10−10–10−5 mol L−1, respectively. The sensor achieved a limit of detection of 0.08 pmol L−1 (fluorescence method) and 2.75 pmol L−1 (colorimetric method, 3σ, n = 9). The developed PCMIP@CD membrane was applied for the detection of sulfadimethoxine in fish samples with favorable recovery of 84.8–102.9%.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Dual-mode detection
Photonic crystal
Molecularly imprinted
Carbon dots
Sulfadimethoxine
Food analysis

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of dispersed SiO2 microsphere and photonic crystal film
Detection of sulfadimethoxine in fish samples
Formation and characterization of photonic crystal structure and PCMIP@CDs film
Analytical performance of dual-mode PCMIP@CD membrane to sulfadimethoxine
Selectivity and repeatability of PCMIP@CDs toward sulfadimethoxine
Analysis of sulfadimethoxine in fish samples

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic diagram of sulfadimethoxine detection based on fluorescence and colorimetric method.
SEM images of PC film with diameters of (A) 368, (B) 400, (C) 460, (D) 495 nm, and (E) PCMIP@CDs film prepared with PC400, (F) the cross-section of PCMIP@CDs.

(A) Fluorescence spectra and (B) linearity of PCMIP@CDs at various concentrations (10−12,10−11,10−10,10−9,10−8,10−7,10−6,10−5 mol L−1) of sulfadimethoxine, (C) Reflection spectra and (D) linearity of PCMIP@CDs membrane at various concentrations (10−10,10−9,10−8,10−7,10−6,10−5 mol L-1) of sulfadimethoxine. F and F0 were the fluorescence intensity of the PCMIP@CDs with and without sulfadimethoxine respectively.

(A) Fluorescence spectra of PCNIP@CDs, and PCMIP@CDs after and before the removal of templates in 0.01 mol L-1 NaH2PO4/Na2HPO4 buffer solution (λex=270 nm), (B) Ultraviolet absorption spectrum of sulfadimethoxine, fluorescence excitation spectra and fluorescence emission spectra of carbon dots in 0.01 mol L-1 NaH2PO4/Na2HPO4 buffer solution.

(A) 3D diagram to demonstrate F0/F of PCMIP@CDs and PCNIP@CDs to sulfadimethoxine, sulfamethazine, sulfadiazine at different concentrations, (B) Reusability of PCMIP@CDs in sulfadimethoxine solution at a concentration of 10−6 mol L−1.
Results of dual-mode method for sulfadimethoxine detection in fish samples.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: The symbol of “–” indicated that it is not detected.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Comparison of the results of different methods based on signal amplification for sulfadimethoxine detection.

head.ce:title: Carbon dot embedded photonic crystal molecularly imprinted as dual-mode fluorometric/colorimetric sensor for the determination of sulfadimethoxine in fish

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: College of Ocean Food and Biological Engineering, Jimei University, Xiamen 361021, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Ocean Food and Biological Engineering, Jimei University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A novel fluorescent membrane based on carbon dot embedded photonic crystal molecularly imprinted polymer (PCMIP@CDs) was developed for fluorometric and colorimetric dual-mode determination of sulfadimethoxine with excellent selectivity, quick response, and reusability. In the presence of sulfadimethoxine, fluorescence quenching and red shift of diffraction peak from PCMIP@CDs were observed because of the photo-induced electron transfer and structural change of PCMIP@CDs, accompanied by a visual color change from blue to green. The fluorescence quenching efficiency and the red-shift of the diffraction peaks were positively correlated with the logarithms of the sulfadimethoxine concentration at 10−12–10−5 and 10−10–10−5 mol L−1, respectively. The sensor achieved a limit of detection of 0.08 pmol L−1 (fluorescence method) and 2.75 pmol L−1 (colorimetric method, 3σ, n = 9). The developed PCMIP@CD membrane was applied for the detection of sulfadimethoxine in fish samples with favorable recovery of 84.8–102.9%.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Sulfonamides are a group of common and widely used antimicrobial agents for the treatment of bacterial infections in aquaculture (Done and Halden, 2015). The abuse of sulfonamides will cause extreme harm to human health. For consumer protection, a maximum residue limit of 100 ng mL−1 for Sulfonamides in edible animal tissues were adopted in some areas by China and the European Commission (Lu et al., 2016). Many analytical methods for sulfadimethoxine detection have been developed, including high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC), capillary electrophoresis (CE), enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA), electrochemical methods, and spectrophotometry (Cai et al., 2022; Dmitrienko et al., 2014; Jamal et al., 2019; Liu et al., 2021; Li et al., 2020). Despite the acceptable sensitivity and accuracy of these methods, some inadequacies are still observed, such as sophisticated instruments, time-consuming procedures, or high cost of testing. Therefore, a new method needs to be developed for the rapid detection of sulfonamide residues.

Photonic crystals are photonic materials with a certain periodical arrangement pattern of dielectric materials to generate a property called photonic bandgap (Cersonsky et al., 2021). According to Bragg’s law, light with certain wavelengths within the bandgap region cannot propagate through PCs, which endows PCs with the structural color property and the slow-photon effect (Hou et al., 2018; Li, et al., 2019). Typically, when the refractive index, lattice constant, and the orderliness of the colloidal particles of PCs are varied to suitable values, a visual change of structural color occurs (Qi et al., 2020). The slow-photon effect can facilitate the interaction between light and matter by significantly retarding light propagation (Chen et al., 2008; Li et al., 2019). An effective method can be used to distinctly enhance fluorescent emission by adjusting electromagnetic wave propagation when the edge of stopband of inverse opal photonic crystals overlaps with the absorption and/or emission band of embedded fluorescent compounds (Ganesh et al., 2007; Li et al., 2011a,2011b). Stimulated by the slow-photon of photonic crystals, a kind of composite solid-state matrix that immobilizes fluorescent sensing agents and photonic crystal framework have been newly reported because of its advantages such as the extraordinary enhancement effect on signal intensity, simplicity of operation and high compatibility with devices (Fan et al., 2021a,2021b). Therefore, this composite matrix is a suitable substrate for dual-mode sensors to improve detection sensitivity.

Considering that food samples contain complex components, and the above-mentioned composite matrix lacks selectivity for analytes, the inclusion of identifying groups is needed to improve the conditions. In recent years, molecularly imprinted polymers (MIP), as bio-inspired synthetic materials that can selectively bind certain target molecules, have been broadly used in food analysis (Ashley et al., 2017; Villa et al., 2021), ultrasensitive analysis (Arabi et al., 2022), chiral discrimination (Arabi et al., 2022), and a new aspect of MIP termed green MIP (Arabi et al., 2021a,2021b; Ostovan et al., 2022; Arabi and Chen, 2022).

The combined application of MIP and photonic crystals can lead to the development of sensors with specific recognition sites, which are expected to achieve rapid detection, especially naked-eye detection in hazardous materials analysis. Wang et al. (2019) developed a PCMIP to visualize the detection of oxytetracycline in milk with a limit of detection (LOD) of 9.6 mmol L−1. A molecularly imprinted magnetically responsive photonic crystal sensor (Xu et al., 2021) was applied for the colorimetric detection of bisphenol A with an LOD of 1 mg L−1, and the response time was 30 s Hong et al. (2020) prepared an Au-MIPC for the visual detection of bisphenol A. The diffraction peak of Au-MIPC was red-shifted in the wavelength range of visible light at bisphenol A concentration of 1 ng mL−1 to 1 mg mL−1, and this phenomenon was accompanied by a structural color change from blue to green. Despite the visual detection, the stability of the Fe3O4 and Au materials used in these methods remained suboptimal and cannot be applied in the practical detection of complex samples. Carbon dots which comprise a class of zero-dimensional nanoparticles with sizes below 10 nm, have shown excellent photochemical stability and simple synthesis process as a fluorescent probe and have been widely used in bio-labeling, optical sensing, and fluorescent sensor (Sobiech et al., 2021; Yuan et al., 2016). (Fan et al., 2021a,2021b) developed a dual-mode fluorescence/colorimetric analysis method based on the non-conjugated polymer carbon dots system for the detection of quercetin in red wine. The fluorescence luminescence performance of the system solution remained stable for several months with an LOD of 8.5 ng mL−1 and recoveries of 90–104.7%. In comparison with single-mode detection, dual-signal detection allows for more reliable results by combining the sensitivity of the fluorescent signal and the visualization of the colorimetric method. Although carbon dots have been used in dual-mode detection methods, to the best of our knowledge, no studies have reported a fluorometric and colorimetric dual-mode method based on carbon dots embedded photonic crystal molecularly imprinted (PCMIP@CDs) membrane for the detection of sulfadimethoxine.

Previous studies (Li et al., 2019; Zhang et al., 2023) about PCMIP are based on the reflectance spectrum signals to identify various sulfonamide. In the present study, a PCMIP@CD membrane for fluorometric and colorimetric detection of sulfadimethoxine. The membrane combined the properties of ordered hierarchical macroporous structure, fast mass transfer, specific adsorption for target molecules, and high fluorescent signal amplification. The fluorescence enhancement of PCMIP@CD membrane on the optimized photonic bandgap can be increased up to 7.1-fold compared with that of the control condition, resulting in a significant fluorescence intensity change. Moreover, the proposed film exhibited a redshift of 80 nm accompanied by a visual color change from blue to green with the addition of sulfadimethoxine. The LOD of the sensor was 0.08 pmol L−1 (3σ, n = 9), which is lower than that of the developed PCMIP membrane. Thereupon, the developed sensor is expected to be applied in the device-free detection of sulfadimethoxine in aquatic products.

Sulfadimethoxine, sulfamethazine, sulfadiazine and azobisisobutyronitrile were obtained from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Tetraethoxysilane, ethylene glycol dimethacrylate, and methacrylic acid were purchased from Aladdin Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Anhydrous citric acid, urea, methanol, ethanol, and acetic acid were obtained from Xilong Chemical Co., Ltd. (Guangdong, China). All chemicals were of analytical grade and were directly used with no further purification. The water (18.2 MΩ cm) used throughout all experiments was purified with TKA Co., Ltd. (Niederelbert, Germany).

Glass microscope slides with dimensions 1 mm × 12 mm × 20 mm were obtained from Xilong Chemical Co., Ltd. (Guangdong, China). Polymethylmethacrylate slides were purchased from Hongjunyuan Insulating Rubber Products Co., Ltd. (Xiamen, China) and cut into the same dimensions as the glass slides.

Fluorescence spectral measurement was performed on a Cary Eclipse fluorescence spectrophotometer (Agilent Technologies Company, Palo Alto, USA). Reflectance spectra were obtained using the MAX2000-Pro fiber optic spectrometer (Wyoptics Co., Ltd., Shanghai, China). The ultraviolet (UV) absorption spectra were obtained using an ultraviolet-visible spectrophotometer (South East Chemicals & Instruments Ltd., Hongkong, China). The Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectrum was measured with ALPHAⅡ FT-IR spectrometer (Bruker Company, Billerica, USA). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) image and scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images were recorded using a JEM-2100 microscope (JEOL, Tokyo, Japan). The photonic crystal growth was performed in an incubator (Yiheng Science Instrument Co. Ltd., Shanghai, China) at a constant temperature of 40 °C and constant humidity of 60%.

Carbon dots were prepared using a facile hydrothermal method (Gao et al., 2016). Briefly, 2.1 g of anhydrous citric acid and 1.8 g of urea were sonically dissolved in 20 mL of deionized water. Then, the solution was transferred to a stainless-steel Teflon-lined vessel, followed by heating at 200 °C for 4 h in an oven. After the reaction, the vessel was cooled to room temperature. Finally, carbon dots were dried under vacuum condition for 72 h and dissolved in methanol with a concentration of 1 g L−1 for further application.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of dispersed SiO2 microsphere and photonic crystal film
ce:section.ce:para: SiO2 particles with various particle sizes (368, 400, 460, and 495 nm) were prepared using the Stöber method, as previously described (Stöber et al., 1968). The prepared silica particles (mass ratio of SiO2 was 0.6%) were dispersed in ethanol to prepare colloidal crystals on a glass microscope slide, acting as substrate. Then, they were placed in an incubator at a constant temperature of 45 °C and constant humidity of 60% for 3 days to build a photonic crystal film after ethanol evaporation.

Approximately 0.02 g of sulfadimethoxine and 0.06 mg carbon dots were dispersed in 1 mL of methanol. The mixture was further added with 0.25 mL of methacrylic acid as the monomer and 0.25 mL of ethylene glycol dimethacrylate as the cross-linker under ultra-sonication. After 10 min, the pre-polymerization solution was placed at 4 °C for 2 h to promote the formation of hydrogen bonds between sulfadimethoxine and methacrylic acid. Next, 0.02 g azobisisobutyronitrile as the initiator, was added while the solution was bubbling with nitrogen atmosphere. Both sides of the photonic crystal film were covered with polymethylmethacrylate plates the same size as the photonic crystal film to form a sandwich plate. Subsequently, the sandwich plates were absorbed in the above mixture until the photonic crystal film became transparent. The sandwich plates were placed in a three-neck flask at 70 °C for 5 h for polymerization.

The PCMIP@CD film was made by soaking the sandwich plates in a 4% HF solution for 1 h, followed by a 1% HF solution for 12 h to remove the photonic crystal framework. Then, the PCMIP@CDs film was immersed in ultra-pure water for 2 h. The target molecules were removed with an eluent prepared using a methanol/acetic acid solution (v:v = 16:1), and then washed with deionized water. The PCMIP@CD film was immersed in a buffer solution of NaH2PO4/Na2HPO4 (0.01 mol L−1, pH 6.0) for further use. The control group photonic crystal molecularly non-imprinted polymer (PCNIP@CD) film was synthesized following the same route as the PCMIP@CD film, except that no target molecules were used in pre-polymerization, while another control group MIP@CD film was prepared in the same manner without photonic crystal structure. The control sample was prepared by drying 0.06 g L−1 carbon dots solution on a quartz glass plate at room temperature and then eluted under the same conditions.

The prepared PCMIP@CD sensor film was placed in a cuvette containing the sulfadimethoxine solution. The film was placed in a manner in which the incident and emissive light were of 45 ° angle to the normal direction of the film, as illustrated in Fig. S1. The fluorescent spectra of each membrane were recorded in the wavelength range of 300–500 nm under the excitation wavelength of 270 nm with a slit width of 5 nm, and the excitation filter parameter was 250–395 nm. The experiments were performed five times, and the average data were calculated. The PCMIP@CD, PCNIP@CD, and MIP@CD films were immersed in the sulfadimethoxine solution with phosphate buffer to measure the fluorescence spectrum of (F). The samples were immersed in 2 mL of phosphate buffer solution to measure F
0 . The quenching efficiencies (F
0
/F) were calculated for further detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of sulfadimethoxine in fish samples
ce:section.ce:para: Gadus samples were purchased from a local supermarket in Xiamen City, China. sulfadimethoxine in fish was extracted based on the Chinese national standard (Determination of 18 sulfonamides residues in pufferfish and eel - liquid chromatography-tandem mass spectrometry, GBT 22951–2008) with some modification. First, approximately 2 g of anhydrous sodium sulfate and 15 mL of acetonitrile were added in 5.0 g samples, and then mixed and homogenized at 3000 rpm for 5 min. In total. 0.1 mL of standard solutions (10−8, 10−7, and 10−6 mol L−1) of sulfadimethoxine were added for spiking. Next, 15 mL of acetonitrile was added to the sample, and the above steps were repeated to transfer the supernatant into a separating liquid funnel. Approximately 20 mL of n-hexane was poured into the separating liquid funnel, and the solution was mixed thoroughly and allowed to stand for 10 min for stratification. The lower-layer liquid was collected and transferred into a spin steaming bottle. The liquid was rotated for evaporation at 45 °C until near dryness. Then, it was dissolved in 10 mL of phosphate buffer solution.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Formation and characterization of photonic crystal structure and PCMIP@CDs film
ce:section.ce:para: The synthesis process and detection method of PCMIP@CDs film are shown in Fig. 1. First, an ordered 3D framework of photonic crystal film was obtained through vertical deposition. Then, the gaps of SiO2 microspheres in the photonic crystal framework were filled by the pre-polymerization solution containing carbon dots via capillary force. After thermal polymerization, SiO2 microspheres and target molecules were removed to form PCMIP@CDs with an inverse opal structure. Upon exposure to the target molecules, the fluorescent intensity of the PCMIP@CD films declined, followed by a visual color change and a decrease in fluorescence intensity.

The SEM images shown in Fig. 2A–D reveal the surface morphology of photonic crystal films made of SiO2 with diameter of 368 nm (PC368, Fig. 2A), 400 nm (PC400, Fig. 2B), 460 nm (PC460, Fig. 2C), and 495 nm (PC495, Fig. 2D). The regular arrangement of SiO2 microspheres in the photonic crystal structure formed a highly ordered structure, which provided a good framework for PCMIPC@CDs with an inverse opal structure. The results show that the fracture surface of photonic crystal film gradually expands with the increase in SiO2 particle size, which is detrimental to the performance of the sensor. Thus, according to the surface topography of photo crystal, SiO2 microspheres with particle size of 368 and 400 nm were chosen to prepare photonic crystal film in subsequent experiments. The surface morphology and cross-section of PCMIPC@CD films prepared with PC400 as templates are shown in Fig. 2E–F. The macroporous structures with their high specific surface are beneficial to the mass transfer of sulfadimethoxine, which is conducive to the improvement of the response speed and sensitivity of the PCMIPC@CD films.

The influence of the photonic crystals’ stopband on the magnitude of fluorescent signal was investigated by measuring the reflection spectra of PCMIP@CD membrane prepared on a glass substrate at an angle of 90° by using a fiber optical spectrophotometer (Fig. S2A). Their fluorescent signals were measured with carbon dot film as the control sample (Fig. S2B). The fluorescence intensities for all PCMIP@CD-based sensors were enhanced compared with the control sample. A 7.1-fold fluorescence enhancement was observed in the blue edge of PCMIP@CDs400 stopband overlapping with carbon dots fluorescence emission peak (421 nm), while a 4.0-fold fluorescence enhancement was observed in the red edge of PCMIP@CDs368 stopband overlapping with carbon dots fluorescence emission peak. The fluorescence enhancement rates of PCMIP@CDs460 (2.4-fold) and PCMIP@CDs495 (1.3-fold) were lower than those of PCMIP@CDs460 and PCMIP@CDs495. Thus, the slow photon effect and the topology of PCMIP@CD membrane contributed to such a high fluorescence amplification efficiency (García et al., 2006; Li et al., 2011a,2011b). The group velocity of the photons became slow at the stopband’s edge, thus inhibiting or suppressing the photon propagation, in which the interaction between light and matter was enhanced (Ganesh et al., 2007; Li et al., 2019; Ogawa et al., 2004). The fluorescence emission peak of carbon dots solution is ∼420 nm, which is near the blue edge of the stopband of PCMIP@CDs400 and the red edge of the stopband of PCMIP@CDs368. Therefore, the blue edge of the stopband is likely to enhance the fluorescence intensity. The silica molding, PCMIP@CDs400, would be the optimal candidate for the preparation of PCMIP@CD film to detect sulfadimethoxine.

The TEM image and FT-IR spectrum of carbon dots are shown in Fig. S3. Based on the morphology, the average particle size of carbon dots is 2–3 nm. A previous study showed that the fluorescence emission peak of small-sized carbon dots is approximately 420 nm (Wang et al., 2018), which is consistent with the fluorescence emission peak of the carbon dots synthesized at 421 nm in this paper ( Fig. 3B). The vibration bands of O–H (3430 cm−1), CN (1400 cm−1), and CO (1585 cm−1) were observed in the IR spectrum, indicating the existence of hydroxyl, carboxyl, and amino groups on the surface of carbon dots. Therefore, carbon dots interact with target and monomer molecules via hydrogen bonds in the fluorescent molecularly imprinted membrane.

The molar proportions of the template, functional monomer, and cross-linking agent, as well as the incorporated carbon dots, affect the selectivity of PCMIP@CDs, while the concentration of carbon dots determines the fluorescence performance of PCMIP@CD films. Herein, the optimum molar proportions of sulfadimethoxine, monomer, and crosslinker were 1:10:4 based on a previously published literature about PCMIP (Lin et al., 2020). Therefore, the number of carbon dots incorporation was optimized according to the quenching efficiency of the PCMIP@CDs and control group PCNIP@CDs in sulfadimethoxine solution at a concentration of 10−6 mol L−1. An optimum amount of carbon dots doping of 0.06 mg mL−1 was determined based on the result in Fig. S4.

The pH of the solution affects the detection sensitivity of PCMIP@CDs, because the pH value of solution determines the charge distribution and the fluorescence properties of the sensor. Therefore, the pH of the detection system was optimized based on the quenching efficiency of the PCMIP@CDs in sulfadimethoxine solution. The optimal pH value was set to 6.0 based on the results shown in Fig. S5.

The effect of the angle between the incident light and the plane of PCMIP@CD film on fluorescence intensity was studied, and the results are shown in Fig. S6A. The optimal measurement angle was set to 45° in the experiment to achieve the highest sensitivity for detection. The fluorescence spectra were measured under different excitation wavelengths, and the maximum fluorescent peak of PCMIP@CDs was observed at the excitation wavelength of 270 nm (Fig. S6B). Herein, the blue-shift phenomenon of all emitted light (emission light of carbon dots at 420 nm according to Fig. 4) can be attributed to the change in the size and surface charge intensity of carbon dots during polymerization and elution (Han et al. 2019, Ma et al., 2019). Therefore, the excitation wavelength of 270 nm was selected for all fluorescence spectra in subsequent experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analytical performance of dual-mode PCMIP@CD membrane to sulfadimethoxine
ce:section.ce:para: The adsorption kinetics curves of PCMIP@CDs, PCNIP@CDs, and MIP@CDs to sulfadimethoxine are shown in Fig. S7. The adsorption reached equilibrium in 30 s, which was shorter than most of the traditional MIPs (over 10 min). The result suggested that PCMIP@CDs with ordered macroporous structure and high specific surface area were conducive to the improvement of the adsorption performance. The fluorescence quenching rate of PCMIP@CDs was higher than that of PCNIP@CDs and MIP@CDs, indicating that the existence of imprinted sites and photonic crystal structure in PCMIP@CDs contributed to the excellent adsorption performance of PCMIP@CDs film.

The effect of sulfadimethoxine concentration on the diffraction peaks and fluorescence intensities of PCMIP@CDs were tested to investigate the linear relationship between them. The fluorescence quenching efficiency of PCMIP@CDs showed an exponential increase with sulfadimethoxine concentration, as shown in Fig. 3. A linear relationship was established between the calibration plot of F
0
/F and the logarithm of sulfadimethoxine concentration based on the equation F
0
/F= 0.1103 lg(C)+ 2.3678 (R
2 =0.987), which is suitable at low sulfadimethoxine concentration range (R
2 =0.989, from 10−12 to 10−8 mol L−1 of sulfadimethoxine). Based on the above phenomenon, the weakened slow photon effect caused by the red-shift of the photonic crystal bandgap accelerated the interaction between light and electrons on the surface of carbon dots, which mainly explains the rapid decline of fluorescence intensity at high sulfadimethoxine concentration (Fig. 3A–C). Another linear relationship obtained by colorimetric method was found to fit the equation of Δλ = 15.7579 lg(C)+ 158.6889 (from 10−10 mol L−1 to 10−5 mol L−1) accompanied by the color change of PCMIP@CD membrane from blue to green.

In this paper, the calculated LODs were 0.08 pmol L−1 (fluorescence method) and 2.75 pmol L−1 (colorimetric method) based on the triple standard deviation method. The LOD of the fluorometric method in this report (0.11 pg g−1) was better than that of HPLC tandem with mass spectrometry (1.0 ng g−1) (Huang et al., 2012). Many signal amplification methods have been applied for the analysis of sulfadimethoxine residues in food.

The fluorescence spectra at ∼380 nm of the PCMIP@CDs and PCNIP@CDs are shown in Fig. 4 A. The fluorescence intensities of PCMIP@CDs with template (before template removal) were significantly lower than those of PCMIP@CDs without template (after template removal), indicating that the presence of template substance will quench the fluorescence of PCMIP@CDs. After removing the template molecules, PCMIP@CDs emitted fluorescence almost the same as PCNIP@CDs, showing that most of the sulfadimethoxine molecules had been eluted. Interestingly, the fluorescence spectra of the eluted PCMIP@CDs exhibited blue-shift of 10 nm compared with those of PCNIP@CDs. The blue-shift phenomenon of the fluorescence spectrum of PCMIP@CDs is possibly related to the change in the surface electronegativity of carbon dots (Ma et al., 2019, Zhou et al., 2017).

The fluorescence quenching of PCMIP@CDs to sulfadimethoxine solution was analyzed using the Stern–Volmer equation (F
0
/F = 1 + K
sv
C
q , where F
0 and F are the fluorescence intensity of the PCMIP@CDs before and after the addition of sulfadimethoxine (analyte), respectively, K
sv is the quenching constant of the quencher, and C
q is the concentration of analyte). The relationship between the fluorescence response (F
0
/F) and all sulfadimethoxine concentrations was not in accordance with the Stern–Volmer equation (Fig. 3B), indicating that the fluorescence quenching mechanism involves the combination of static and dynamic fluorescence quenching. The sensing mechanism of PCMIP@CD membrane was further studied by determining the absorption spectrum of sulfadimethoxine, and the excitation and emission spectra of carbon dots. As shown in Fig. 4B, no intersection can be observed between the UV absorption of sulfadimethoxine and the fluorescence spectrum of carbon dots. Moreover, no obvious fluorescence quenching was observed when mixing the carbon dots solution with sulfadimethoxine (Fig. S8). Based on the results of the above experiments, the dynamic fluorescence quenching can be mainly ascribed to photo-induced electron transfer (Cui et al., 2020). In the presence of sulfadimethoxine, the charge of lone pair electrons of the carboxyl groups on the surface of the fluorescent carbon dots in PCMIP@CDs may be transferred to the hydrogen bonds of sulfadimethoxine, resulting in fluorescence quenching (de Silva et al., 2009, Jung et al., 2016; Liu et al., 2020). In summary, the combination of photo-induced electron transfer of PCMIP@CDs and the static fluorescence quenching leads to the fluorescence quenching of carbon dots.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity and repeatability of PCMIP@CDs toward sulfadimethoxine
ce:section.ce:para: sulfamethazine and sulfadiazine with similar chemical structures as sulfadimethoxine, were selected to evaluate the selectivity of PCMIP@CDs at the concentration range of 10−12–10−5 mol L−1. As shown in Fig. 5A, the quenching efficiency of sulfadimethoxine was significantly greater than those of sulfamethazine and sulfadiazine at the same concentrations for PCMIP@CDs. By contrast, the change in the fluorescence intensity of PCNIP@CDs to sulfadimethoxine, sulfamethazine, and sulfadiazine was almost the same. The results also confirmed that the PCMIP@CD film had excellent selectivity for sulfadimethoxine because of the presence of specific binding cavities.

The fluorescence intensity changes of PCMIP@CD film were recorded to evaluate reusability, and the results are shown in Fig. 5B. The fluorescence intensity of PCMIP@CDs film decreased after each cycle of adsorption/elution. Given the structural damage of PCMIP@CD film (Fig. S9) after elution, the fluorescence intensity and quenching efficiency decreased, and the performance was worse than that of the first one. However, after three cycles of usage, approximately 90% of fluorescence quenching efficiency of PCMIP@CDs was still maintained.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of sulfadimethoxine in fish samples
ce:section.ce:para: The PCMIP@CD film was applied to determine sulfadimethoxine residues in fish, and the recoveries of sulfadimethoxine were tested by spiking sulfadimethoxine into the fish samples to investigate the practicality of the method. The reliability of this method was evaluated using a commercial kit purchased from Quanzhou Ruixin Biotechnology Co., Ltd. (China) as a reference method. The reference method is based on the indirect competitive ELISA method (detailed procedure is listed in the Supporting information) for the detection of sulfonamides in tissues. The results in Table 1 show that the recoveries ranged from 87.1% to 98.4% for the colorimetric method and from 95.7% to 108.1% for the fluorescence method with relative standard deviation of less than 10%. The analytical indexes of dual-mode method were close to those of the reference method, and no target was detected in the reference method at 10−8 mol L−1 sulfadimethoxine. These results confirmed the stability and accuracy of our dual-mode method. The analytical indexes of these reports are listed in Table 2 for comparison. The proposed sensor in this work was capable of the dual-mode detection of sulfadimethoxine and exhibited improved indexes at LOD and linear range.

Herein, a novel fluorescent film of PCMIP@CDs was developed with excellent selectivity and quick responsiveness, and it was characteristic of the dual-mode detection of colorimetry and fluorescence. Benefiting from the slow photon effect of photonic crystal and the specificity of MIP, the fluorescent signal of carbon dots on the substrate of PCMIP increased by 7.1-fold, resulting in a significant fluorescence quenching for the detection of sulfadimethoxine. The dual mode of the sensor film combining the fluorescence method and the colorimetric method offers the possibility of rapid, device-free detection for sulfadimethoxine with an LOD of as low as 0.08 pmol L−1 and was successfully used for the detection of fish samples with recoveries of 84.8–102.9%. Therefore, the method developed in this paper is expected to be implemented in the label-free detection of sulfadimethoxine in fish products.

Shishun Zhang: Experiment, Data analysis, Writing – original draft. Keman Shao: Partly participate in material preparation, Specimen collection. Chengyi Hong: Project instructor, Methodology, Conceptualization. Suyan Chen: Project instructor, Methodology. Zhengzhong Lin: Methodology, Writing – review & editing, Data curation, Validation. Zhiyong Huang: Conceptualization, Project administration. Gulinigaer Mureti: Software, Investigation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declared that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was supported by the Foundation from the Natural Science Foundation of Fujian Province of China (2023J01778), the Open Foundation from Key Laboratory of Food Microbiology and Enzyme Engineering of Fujian Province (Z820224-3), and the National Undergraduate Training Programs for Innovation and Entrepreneurship (2023xj003).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.jfca.2023.105477.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
Supplementary material.
.

Sensors and Actuators B-Chemical, 338, 129858. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2021.129858.
